profile.summary: A Junior Graphic Designer supports day-to-day visual work across marketing, brand, digital, and sometimes print. They usually execute within an existing brand system, prepare assets, make revisions, and learn through feedback from more senior designers, marketers, and stakeholders. Their scope is mostly delivery-focused, with growing ownership of small projects and increasing responsibility for file quality, consistency, and speed.

known_challenges.text: Turning loose or changing briefs into clear visual work without much context.
Building speed and confidence while still needing frequent feedback and direction.
Handling rounds of revisions without taking critique personally.
Keeping files, exports, and brand details accurate across many small tasks.
Balancing production work with chances to grow concept and craft skills.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Clarity of briefs and handoff quality
recommended_question_themes.why: Junior designers often struggle most when requests are vague, rushed, or missing key constraints.
How they receive and use feedback strongly affects growth, morale, and output quality at this level.
This role develops through repetition in layout, typography, hierarchy, and brand consistency.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Workload mix between production and learning
recommended_question_themes.why: Too much repetitive asset work can stall development and reduce motivation.
Junior designers need help learning how to clarify requests, explain choices, and manage changes calmly.

terminology.meaning: Rules for using logos, colors, type, and imagery.
How type is chosen, spaced, and arranged.
Visual order that guides attention through content.
How elements are arranged on a page or screen.
Saving files in the right format, size, and settings.
Structured feedback on work and design choices.
A cycle of requested changes to a design.
Passing final files or specs to another team.
Keeping track of file changes and latest edits.
The core goal, audience, message, and constraints.
Adapting existing designs for new content.
Work saved to show skills and growth.
Careful visual accuracy in spacing and alignment.
Preparing final assets accurately and efficiently.

listen_for: They talk mostly about making requested changes, not yet about why choices work.
They mention unclear briefs or late feedback as blockers.
They describe critique in emotional terms, which can signal confidence strain.
They focus on file prep, resizing, and asset volume, showing a production-heavy workload.
They start using craft terms like hierarchy or typography more precisely over time.
avoid: Assuming fast execution means they need little guidance.
Treating revision-heavy work as low-skill or not worth discussing.
Pushing full creative ownership before they have enough context and support.
Giving vague feedback like 'make it pop' instead of concrete direction.
